positioning.name: SecurityRecipes client-side marketplace
positioning.summary: A BYO-token browser control plane for AI security work: connect context, apply recipes, import scans, generate normalized reports, and hand results to downstream systems without server-side secret storage.
positioning.browser_model: Provider credentials, connector settings, and selected workflow state stay in browser storage. Recipes and marketplace templates are open Hugo content. Live API calls are explicit and same-origin or direct-to-provider from the browser.
positioning.contribution_model: Marketplace entries are Hugo data files and docs content so teams can fork, contribute, review, and publish new channels and workflows through normal pull requests.

market_signals.signal: Security teams increasingly expect scanner outputs to land in a common interchange format, which makes browser-side SARIF and JSON normalization a practical baseline instead of a niche feature.
Enterprises now expect large governed integration catalogs with change detection, version pinning, and audit logging.
Security teams want AI investigation and remediation surfaces that can slot into existing pipelines instead of forcing a greenfield workflow.

market_signals.source: Wiz State of AI in the Cloud 2026
market_signals.signal: AI is now core operational infrastructure, 2026 is the year of integration, and agents plus MCP servers have become a new control-plane attack surface that needs explicit governance.
Security teams still spend large amounts of time on repetitive manual work, which increases the value of normalized report bundles and reusable workflow templates that fit existing handoff systems.
APIs, MCP servers, and data access now form one attack surface, so scan/report/output contracts need to be explicit and inspectable.
The browser is a meaningful AI operating surface, which supports a client-side BYO-token model but requires clear guardrails around extensions, personal accounts, and data egress.
